instruction: In switch 1, any PING request destined for 10.0.0.1 should go through port 3.
In switch 1, forward TCP traffic on port 80 destined for 10.0.0.3 through port 2.
In switch 1, forward all TCP traffic not matching higher-priority rules through port 1.
Forward traffic entering on port 1 of switch 2 to port 2.
In switch 4, block all IPv4 traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 with a high priority, ensuring the switch operates as a firewall.
In switch 4, forward all traffic not matching higher-priority rules through port 1.
Forward TCP traffic on port 80 destined for 10.0.0.3 via interface 2 of switch 1, assigning it to queue 0 for prioritized handling.
Forward UDP traffic on port 80 destined for 10.0.0.3 via interface 2, assigning it to queue 1 for prioritized handling in switch 1.
In switch 1, forward ICMP (ping) packets destined to 10.0.0.1 out through port 3.
In node 1, route HTTP traffic (TCP port 80) with IPv4 destination 10.0.0.3/32 via interface 2.
In switch 1, route default TCP traffic via interface 1.
Route all packets received on port 1 of node 2 to interface 2.
Prioritize blocking packets from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 to enforce firewall rules effectively in node 4.
Route default traffic of node 4 via interface 1.
For node 1, route HTTP (TCP port 80) traffic targeting 10.0.0.3/32 through port 2 with traffic assigned to queue 0.
In switch 1, route UDP traffic (port 80) targeting 10.0.0.3/32 through port 2 with traffic assigned to queue 1.
Direct traffic with IPv4 destination 10.0.0.1/32 in node 1 through interface 3.
Ensure TCP packets on port 80 of node 1 addressed to 10.0.0.3 are sent through port 2.
Send non-specific TCP traffic arriving at node 4 through port 1.
Direct incoming traffic from port 1 of node 3 out via port 2.
Block all IPv4 traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 in openflow 4.
In openflow switch 1, send non-specific traffic through port 1.
Ensure TCP packets on destination port 80 addressed to 10.0.0.3 are forwarded via port 2 using queue 0 of switch 1.
For openflow 1, ensure UDP packets on destination port 80 addressed to 10.0.0.3 are forwarded via port 2 using queue 1.
Ensure packets matching Ethernet type 0x0800 and destination 10.0.0.1 in switch 1 are sent via port 3.
Direct TCP traffic (port 80) targeting 10.0.0.3 out via port 2 in node 1.
In switch 1, direct unspecified TCP traffic to connector 1.
Deny packets originating from 10.0.0.1 destined for 10.0.0.4 in node 4.
Direct unspecified traffic to connector 1 in switch 1.
Direct TCP traffic on port 80 intended for 10.0.0.3 out through port 2, assigning it to queue 0 in node 1.
Using openflow 1, direct UDP traffic on port 80 intended for 10.0.0.3 out through port 2, assigning it to queue 1.
Implement a static routing rule in node 1 to forward IPv4 traffic addressed to 10.0.0.1 to port 3.
Drop all packets with a source IP of 10.0.0.1 and destination IP of 10.0.0.4 using node 4.
Block communication between 10.0.0.1 and 10.0.0.4 using switch 4.
Route TCP traffic entering switch 1 on port 1 and targeted at port 80 through interface 2, placing it in queue 0 for priority handling.
In switch 2, redirect all SSH traffic (TCP port 22) destined for 10.0.0.5 through port 4.
In switch 3, assign high-priority queue 2 to video streaming traffic (UDP port 8080) destined for 10.0.0.6 through interface 3.
Forward all ICMP echo replies destined for 10.0.0.2 through port 5 of node 2 for network diagnostics.
Drop all traffic from 10.0.0.9 on switch 2 while forwarding all other traffic normally.
Using openflow 2, forward IPv6 traffic (Ethernet type 0x86DD) destined for fe80::1 through port 2.
Forward non-IP traffic (Ethernet type 0x0806) to port 8 for processing ARP requests using node 2.
At switch 1, route multicast traffic (224.0.0.1/32) through port 9 with queue 0 for prioritized handling.
Block TCP traffic from 192.168.2.2 destined for 10.0.0.10 on port 443 of node 2 to prevent unauthorized HTTPS access.
In switch 2, allow only UDP traffic from 10.0.0.11 to 10.0.0.12 on port 69 (TFTP) through port 6.
Using switch number 2, block all outgoing traffic from 10.0.0.13 while allowing incoming traffic to be routed through port 7.
Ensure all broadcast traffic (destination 255.255.255.255) is forwarded through port 10 of openflow switch 2.
Prioritize routing SNMP traffic (UDP port 161) to port 5 using queue 0 of switch 4.

Route HTTP traffic originating from 192.168.1.2 on port 1 of switch 4 and destined for 10.0.0.5/32 through port 2, ensuring packets are assigned to queue 0 for low-latency processing and apply VLAN tag 100.
In switch 3, create a slice for IoT devices. Route MQTT traffic (TCP port 1883) from 192.168.3.10 to 10.0.0.20/32 via port 2 and assign it to queue 1 for high-priority processing.
Using openflow 1, SIP signaling traffic (UDP 5060) coming from 192.168.1.50 (a VoIP server) to 10.0.0.100 (a VoIP client) should be assigned queue 3 for prioritization and forwarded via port 4.

Give h4 DNS traffic to h2 at least 2 Mbps.
Keep h3 to h1 ICMP below 10 ms.
Guarantee five megabits from h1 to h3.
Keep h2-to-h4 delay under twenty milliseconds.
Put web traffic from h1 to h3 on queue one.
Guarantee two megabits for DNS from h4 to h2.
Keep pings from h3 to h1 under ten milliseconds.
Make the network better.

Route HTTP from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 through the firewall on switch 1 port 9.
Send all traffic from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.4 via the firewall on switch 1.
Forward SSH traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 through port 9 of switch 1 for inspection.
Inspect ICMP from 10.0.0.3 to 10.0.0.1 using the security function at switch 1 port 9.
Route DNS traffic from 10.0.0.4 to 10.0.0.2 through the middlebox on port 9 of switch 1.
All HTTPS traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 must pass through the firewall at switch 1 port 9.
Redirect FTP from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.3 through the inspection device on switch 1 port 9.
Enforce traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 to pass through port 9 of switch 1.
Send UDP traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.2 via security function at switch 1 port 9.
Force all traffic from 10.0.0.4 to 10.0.0.1 through the firewall at port 9 of switch 1.
Route HTTP from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 through switch 2 for deep packet inspection.
Forward all traffic from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.4 via switch 2 as the IDS node.
Send TCP port 443 traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 through switch 2 for load balancing.
Route ICMP from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 through switch 3 for monitoring.
Chain all traffic from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.3 through switch 2 then switch 4.
Apply IDS on switch 2 for HTTP traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4, then deliver.
Forward DNS from 10.0.0.3 to 10.0.0.1 via switch 3 for logging.
Route all UDP from 10.0.0.4 to 10.0.0.2 through switch 3.
Send FTP from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 through switch 2 and switch 4.
Apply firewall on switch 1 then IDS on switch 2 for 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 traffic.
Inspect traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 through port 9 of switch 1; drop if malicious.
Route SMTP from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.3 through the firewall; block if it fails inspection.
Chain HTTP traffic from 10.0.0.1 through switch 2 IDS; forward clean traffic to 10.0.0.4.
Apply security inspection on switch 1 port 9 for all traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4.
Send SSH from 10.0.0.1 through firewall at switch 1 port 9 before allowing to 10.0.0.4.
Reroute traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 via switch 2 instead of switch 3.
Redirect all traffic from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.4 through switch 3 if switch 2 is down.
Send 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 traffic via switch 3 to avoid switch 2 congestion.
Reroute 10.0.0.3 to 10.0.0.1 packets through switch 2 to bypass switch 3.
Redirect HTTP traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 via switch 2 for maintenance on switch 3.
Reroute ICMP from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.4 via switch 3 instead of switch 2.
Redirect all traffic from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 to use switch 2 path.
Bypass switch 3: reroute 10.0.0.4 to 10.0.0.1 via switch 2.
Reroute DNS from 10.0.0.4 to 10.0.0.2 through switch 2 instead of switch 3.
Force 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.3 traffic to take the switch 3 path, not switch 2.
On switch 1, redirect traffic destined for 10.0.0.4 from egress port 2 to port 1.
Change output port for 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 HTTP traffic on switch 1 to port 2.
Reroute traffic entering port 1 of switch 2 to exit on port 2.
Redirect 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.4 traffic on switch 1 to use port 2 instead of port 1.
On switch 4, change egress port for 10.0.0.3 to 10.0.0.1 traffic from port 1 to port 2.
Switch 1: redirect HTTP from 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 to go out on port 1.
Reroute UDP DNS traffic on switch 1 to exit port 2 instead of port 1.
On switch 4, redirect traffic to 10.0.0.1 to port 2 instead of port 1.
If switch 3 fails, redirect all 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 traffic through switch 2.
Failover: send 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.4 via switch 2 when switch 3 link is unavailable.
Emergency reroute: bypass switch 2 and send 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.3 traffic via switch 3.
Reroute all traffic on switch 4 port 1 to exit via port 2 during maintenance.
Redirect 10.0.0.3 to 10.0.0.1 traffic from switch 4 via switch 2 if switch 3 is unreachable.
Primary path down: reroute 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.4 traffic through switch 2 immediately.
Backup path activation: send all TCP traffic from 10.0.0.2 to 10.0.0.4 via switch 3.
